food_name: Sambal Kangkong (Water Spinach in Sambal Belacan)
aliases: wok-fried water spinach with shrimp paste

english: wok-fried water spinach with shrimp paste

food_subgroup: Stir-fried leafy vegetables with sambal sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a water spinach entry (FOODON_03315290) but no specific entry for the sambal kangkong dish preparation. Closest parent applied.
ontology.foodex2_description: Water spinach — closest FoodEx2 parent for stir-fried water spinach with sambal belacan
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for sambal kangkong as a prepared dish. A0VGS (water spinach) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for sambal kangkong or water spinach dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard zi char restaurant serving of sambal kangkong is approximately 180g, comprising roughly 150g of blanched or wok-fried water spinach and 30g of sambal belacan paste.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Water Spinach (Ipomoea aquatica), cooked; supplemented with HPB Singapore nutrient data for sambal belacan paste composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central) and Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central cooked water spinach data combined with HPB Singapore sambal belacan nutritional composition. Dish-level values represent a weighted blend of ~83% water spinach (cooked) and ~17% sambal belacan paste by weight. Values are consistent with the input record which carries verified/canonical provenance.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of high-fibre leafy vegetables — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked leafy greens and low-starch vegetable dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sambal kangkong in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of approximately 30 is estimated by analogy with cooked high-fibre leafy green vegetables (GI range 15–35 per University of Sydney GI Database). The dish is predominantly water spinach (high fibre, low starch) with a small sambal belacan paste component contributing approximately 3g sugar per serving. GL per 180g serving = (30 × 9.9g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 2.6 (very low). Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~380mg/100g (~684mg per 180g serving) from belacan (fermented shrimp paste)
hypertension.guidance: Sambal kangkong delivers approximately 684mg sodium per serving, primarily from belacan; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced belacan, noting that this dish remains the lowest-sodium zi char vegetable option compared to oyster-sauce alternatives (~850–900mg sodium per serving).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic load (GL ~2.6 per serving) and high dietary fibre (~4g per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 30 and a GL of only 2.6 per 180g serving, sambal kangkong is an excellent zi char side dish choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the 4g dietary fibre per serving slows gastric emptying and attenuates postprandial glucose response when consumed alongside white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.7g/100g), high Vitamin A (400mcg RAE/100g), high Vitamin C (27mg/100g), and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sambal kangkong is a cardioprotective vegetable dish with low saturated fat, negligible trans fat, and high antioxidant micronutrients (Vitamin A ~400mcg RAE and Vitamin C ~27mg per 100g); the moderate sodium content (~684mg per serving) warrants mindful consumption for individuals managing cardiovascular risk, but the overall lipid and phytonutrient profile is favourable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High Vitamin A (beta-carotene precursor) and Vitamin C with anti-inflammatory phytonutrient profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high Vitamin A content (~400mcg RAE/100g as beta-carotene) and Vitamin C (~27mg/100g) in sambal kangkong support collagen synthesis and provide anti-inflammatory antioxidant activity, making it a suitable and beneficial side dish for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Kangkong is a classic Singaporean zi char and Malay hawker side dish consisting of water spinach (Ipomoea aquatica, also known as kangkong, morning glory, or ong choy) stir-fried or blanched at high heat and tossed with sambal belacan paste — a pungent, spicy, umami condiment made from toasted fermented shrimp paste (belacan), dried chillies, shallots, garlic, lime juice, and sugar. The dish has a tender-crisp texture with hollow stems and soft leaves, carrying a bold, savoury-spicy flavour with characteristic fermented shrimp umami. Per 180g serving: approximately 112 kcal, 5g protein, 6.3g fat (1.3g saturated), 9.9g carbohydrate, 4g dietary fibre, and 684mg sodium. Vitamin A is very high at ~720mcg RAE per serving (an excellent source). GI is approximately 30 (low) with a glycemic load of only 2.6 per serving — one of the lowest GL zi char dishes available. CRITICAL ALLERGEN: belacan contains shellfish (shrimp); unsuitable for crustacean/shellfish-allergic individuals. Dietary flags: halal, gluten-free, dairy-free. The dish is a staple across Singapore zi char restaurants, Malay hawker stalls, and seafood restaurants, and is widely regarded as one of the most nutritious and lowest-calorie zi char vegetable options. Sources: USDA FoodData Central (cooked water spinach) and HPB Singapore (sambal belacan composition).
meal_context: Singapore zi char restaurant side dish
lunch or dinner accompaniment to white rice

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from USDA FoodData Central (cooked water spinach, an approved primary source) combined with HPB Singapore sambal belacan composition data (approved primary regional source). The input record carries verified/canonical provenance with confidence 1.0. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked high-fibre leafy greens, which is the approved GI source. All values are internally consistent and plausible for this dish type.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the sambal kangkong dish as prepared is not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database at the dish level. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the combined dish is not reported in the cited sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked water spinach and sambal belacan components). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for this plant-based dish with minimal animal-derived components per USDA FoodData Central). GI value of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for sambal kangkong exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Shellfish allergen (belacan) is critical and must be flagged at point of service. Dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to belacan (fermented shrimp paste). The hypertension concern level is moderate due to ~684mg sodium per serving; this is nonetheless the lowest-sodium zi char vegetable option compared to oyster-sauce preparations.
